en.extract:
The dark-eyed white-eye (Zosterops tetiparius) is a species of passerine bird in the family Zosteropidae. It has two subspecies, Z. t. tetiparius and Z. t. paradoxus.


Habitat
Zosterops tetiparius is found in subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest in the Solomon Islands.
Z. t. paradoxus is found on Rendova Island and Z. t. tetiparius on Tetepare Island. These islands lie just 3.4km apart from each other. The two subspecies have never been recorded away from their respective home islands.
Z. tetiparius is non-migratory.


Description
Like other white-eye species, Zosterops tetiparius is yellow-olive in color, with a slender bill. Z. t. tetiparius can be distinguished by its pale belly. Z. tetiparius lacks the white eye ring that gives the white-eye family its name.


Conservation status
The dark-eyed white-eye is classified as Least Concern by the IUCN, although its exact population is unknown. Due to its small native range, it is thought to be at risk due to random events such as hurricanes.

The '''dark-eyed white-eye''' ('''''Zosterops tetiparius''''') is a species of [[passerine]] [[bird]] in the family [[Zosteropidae]]. It has two subspecies, ''Z. t. tetiparius'' and ''Z. t. paradoxus''.

== Habitat ==
''Zosterops tetiparius'' is found in [[subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest]] in the [[Solomon Islands]].

''Z. t. paradoxus'' is found on [[Rendova Island]] and ''Z. t. tetiparius'' on Tetepare Island.<ref name="IBC">{{cite web|author=Internet Bird Collection|url=http://ibc.lynxeds.com/species/solomon-white-eye-zosterops-kulambangrae|title=Solomon White-eye (Zosterops kulambangrae)|accessdate=9 January 2010}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=Mees, G. F.|year=1955|url=http://www.repository.naturalis.nl/document/150019|title=The name of the white-eye from Rendova Island (Solomon Islands)|journal=Zoologische Mededelingen|volume=33|issue=28|pages=99–100}}</ref> These islands lie just 3.4km apart from each other. The two subspecies have never been recorded away from their respective home islands.<ref>{{cite book|author=Mayr, Ernst & Jared M. Diamond|year=2001|title=The Birds of Northern Melanesia: Speciation, Ecology and Biogeography|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=AUUQsAuk9qsC&dq=%22zosterops+rendovae%22&pg=PA492|publisher=Oxford University Press | isbn=978-0-19-514170-2}}</ref>

''Z. tetiparius'' is non-migratory.<ref name="iucn">{{Cite journal |last=BirdLife International (BirdLife International) |date=2023-07-18 |title=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species: Zosterops tetiparius |url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/232637624/232767305 |journal=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species |archive-url=http://web.archive.org/web/20250815025849/https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/232637624/232767305 |archive-date=2025-08-15}}</ref>

== Description ==
Like other white-eye species, ''Zosterops tetiparius'' is yellow-olive in color, with a slender bill. ''Z. t. tetiparius'' can be distinguished by its pale belly. ''Z. tetiparius'' lacks the white eye ring that gives the white-eye family its name.<ref name="cornell">{{Cite journal |last=van Balen |first=Bas |date=2021 |title=Dark-eyed White-eye (Zosterops tetiparius), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/soiwhe3/cur/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.soiwhe3.01 |issn=2771-3105}}</ref>

== Conservation status ==
The dark-eyed white-eye is classified as Least Concern by the [[IUCN]], although its exact population is unknown.<ref name="iucn"></ref> Due to its small native range, it is thought to be at risk due to random events such as hurricanes.<ref name="cornell"></ref>
